speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm rolling. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue our story right now--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, sir.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the events that are going on now that you're working for the gendarmerie, was anybody unkind to you, or did you experience any-- anybody hitting you or any brutality? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: No. As long as we did what we are supposed to do, they left us alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about on the home scene?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the home scene, people were disappearing. My cousin disappeared one-- one day. We didn't know where he was, and so were other people disappearing. We didn't know what-- where they were. They were just grabbing people and taking them out of town and shooting them. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anybody being grabbed like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see them, but I know my cousin disappeared and never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're working. And meanwhile at home, people were just disappearing. They're taking people away, and where did they shoot them? On the outskirts of town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Take them outskirts of town and shoot them in the outskirts of town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a nice Jewish population there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes there was about 15,000 Jews there in Dubno.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many are they taking away a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: About five to 10 a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody else who wasn't Jewish? Were they were taking them away?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you could really feel the difference between being Jewish and not Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the Ukrainians and the townspeople? How did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were bad. They sided up with the Germans right away. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were great in recognizing Jews, where the Germans couldn't recognize a Jew, who was a Jew and who wasn't. So they-- they would go around with the Germans and point out this is a Jew and this is a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so they would then take them to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. Ah, either they'd take him out and shoot him, or they send them away to concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, now it's 1941. The Germans have invaded, and they are now in control. You're working for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Gendarmarie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened? How long were you there? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the end of 1941, when-- when I saw that they were bringing in the Ukrainian police from-- from the outskirts, from the surrounding areas and getting ready for some actions, whatever they-- I told my cousin, I said, I don't like what's going on there. I'm going to go run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my parents knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they say?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your brothers and sisters at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were home, and they couldn't go with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you decided to run away and become a Partisan?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know what I was going to be, but being in the woods, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I encountered a Partisan group, and I-- I joined them. I joined them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about your last night at home before you ran away.

speaker: interviewee
text: I embraced my family and said goodbye. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see them again after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never saw them again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left the next day, and you went into the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you meet up with?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of young Jewish kids, they ran away also. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go by yourself then, or did you go with a friend? Or who did you go--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we went with a group. We had to have the-- the help from the Jewish police. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a group of about 50 to 100 youngsters that were running away, and we couldn't just go out and run. We had to make it look that we are going to work. To the outskirts of town, they had a-- a factory of sausages that they used to make sausages there. And-- and the Germans used to take a lot of people from-- from the ghetto to work there in-- in that factory. So we-- we had to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] go in formation. And the Jewish police escorted us. And when it came on the outskirts of town, we had to go to the factory to the right. We started going to the left. We went to the left. Then we ran into the woods. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? Did people start shooting at you? Or what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened-- when I worked for the gendarmerie they used to pick up weapons that the Russians threw away-- threw away. And they used to store them in a second part of the garage. So I told my cousin we better get a couple of rifles here before we go. So I picked up a couple of rifles. And I took-- I had a little two-wheeler with a boxed in box on top. And I used to take home [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the sawings from the wood. They used to allow me take it home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the sawdust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sawdust. So and I-- I put a couple of rifles on one of those two-wheelers. One-- the short one was OK. But the long one, I couldn't get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: It stuck out at the top?

speaker: interviewee
text: It stuck out. So I put some-- I put some rags around it, and I-- I went to the ghetto with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went back to the ghetto with the guns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how many guns did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two. I hid them. I hid them There were-- there were the-- also Jewish kids working-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a-- [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] in a military post, which they-- they took the guns and they cut it-- cut off half-- half of the pipe and a  piece of the wood. They used to take them under their clothes and-- and bring them home. So all-in-all, they had about 100 rifles and 100 guns like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we never had any experience what to-- how to maintain them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever shoot a gun before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never shot a gun before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was it like the first time you shot a gun?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you're in the woods now, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the woods. And the people that were supposed to take care of us, all they told us-- we're going to go to a-- a number 15 [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] section. We're going to go to number 15 section. So we got to the woods. And it got night, and everybody wanted to sleep. We fell asleep. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We fell asleep, and in the morning we got up. All those fellows that were supposed to take us to section 15 weren't there anymore, and the guns weren't there anymore. So we had to do the best we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we watched the road, and when we saw some farmer going by there, we ran out, and we tried to buy some food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of those guys said, you don't have to buy anything. You wait here, and I'll buy you-- I'll bring you some food. He brought a-- a bucket full of soup. And we had that soup and existed the best way we could. We went on the fields and stole some potatoes and carrots from the fields and fed ourselves with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this group of 100 fellows and you were trying to do this. So then what happens? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then that wasn't enough for these Ukrainians, the Banderovcis, that wasn't enough. So they kept attacking us in the woods when we were in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, explain who the Banderovcis were.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Banderovcis were the Ukrainians. They were fighting for an independent Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were sort of like Ukrainian Partisans. Yeah, they were Ukrainian Partisans, but for their own cause, to-- to get a country of their own. And they were still fighting against the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, tell me about the Bulbovcis.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Bulbovcis-- they were the same kind of group, but different leaders. The Bulbovcis had  a different leader, and the Banderovcis had a different leader. They were two different groups, but they were all Ukrainians, and they were all trying to clear out the area free of Jews. And they kept on attacking us in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're fighting a war against the Ukrainians and the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you-- when you talk about the fighting, did you ever wind up getting any guns, or how did you protect yourself? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like  I said, to begin with, we had about 50-- 50 guns with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happens-- you said that they stole the guns at night. You still had 50 guns left over? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: At a later time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I met-- I met one leader also from Polish resistance, and he-- he-- he knew my father, and he-- he-- he knew my younger brother. And I told him about it. So he called in one of the fellows that were with him. Shuhart, come here. Show him that gun. So he came in with my gun. I said, that's my gun. He said, give him-- give him back the gun. So he gave me back my gun. And then we were on our own already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the leader of your group? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was more or less the leader there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- did they give you a nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: The nickname came later when I was in the Partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're now in the woods. It's what, 1941, '42?

speaker: interviewer
text: You're in the woods, and you're dealing with the elements. You're trying to stay alive, but you're not necessarily involved in any resistance movements. You're just trying to stay alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. We just tried to stay alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any-- anything happening to anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they attacked our camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukrainians attacked our camp. And the two fellows that were left behind watching the camp-- they killed those two fellows.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Ukrainians killed the two fellows?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what happened? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to the fellows, this is-- this is no good. We got to do something about it. We got to organize a stronger-- a stronger group and let's attack them. Instead of they attacking us, let's attack them. So we found out where they were keeping themselves, and we attacked them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about the attack.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went into the village where they were-- where they were stationed there. And we found out in what house they were housed, and we surrounded the house. We start telling them, come out. Put your hands up or we-- we're going to burn you out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: If-- if they came out. We didn't-- we didn't shoot there. We just-- we just had a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: Conversation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Conversation, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, did you get into any kind of a struggle with them, or what? Did anybody get killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What? Someone got killed, or you had  a struggle? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in one camp, six of my people got killed. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They just came, and they start shooting at our camp without any warning. We were fighting back. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] After this was all going on, there-- there was a Russian Partisan group over there formed itself. And the local population reported to the Russians that there is a Jewish group in your Partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one of the Russian leaders came over, and he asked him if he want to join them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I and another fellow, Yoste Shuhart, joined them. And we were now under the Russian Partisans under the Russian command. Whatever they had to do, we did it. So now you're officially unofficially a Partisan with the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. So we were blowing up bridges and blowing up [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] railroads that they were sending [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the front, supplies to the front. So we were blowing it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to learn about explosives?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have to learn because it was already-- all they had to do was pull a string and that ignited the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Explosives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Explosives. But the explosives were ready. All you had to do was pull a string to ignite it. So we picked a-- we picked a-- a spot where it would do the best damage for-- for the railroads. And we went there. We dug this under the railroad tracks. And we stood back about 5,000 feet when-- when we heard a train going by that area. So we pulled the string and blew up-- blew up the track and the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Must have felt good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it felt very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it dangerous work?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was dangerous work. That's how I got my-- my nickname, Nieustrazony Rozpoznawczy, because I did not fear any-- anything they were sending me out to do, I went with pleasure to do it. I got my nickname Nieustrazony Rozpoznawczy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means fearless reconnaissance, fearless reconnaissance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you told me the first time that you brought home a gun and shot it that you were afraid. And all of a sudden, now, you're a fearless--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when you do it over and over again, you get used to it like anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever shoot anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not-- not for the sake of shooting. I shoot-- I shot some people in a battle, in a war, like the Banderovcis. They-- they surrounded us, and they wanted to chase us out of that area where we were-- where we were stationed, we had our camp. And we had only about 200 people at one time. They surrounded us with 600 people strong. So we were fighting them. And we made our way out of that--

speaker: interviewer
text: That area?

speaker: interviewee
text: That area. And we went to a different area.

speaker: interviewer
text: What area was that specifically? Do you know what it was called?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called Turemne, Turemne. T-U-R-E-M-N-E. Turemne. There was a woods where they had-- we had water. That was a water spout coming there. So we had water there, and we kept our camp there. Then we had another camp for the winter. We had pine trees in that camp for the winter because you could-- with an airplane you could see, you know, the Germans used to fly over that area. And when they see something going on there, they used to throw bombs. So we-- in the wintertime, we had to go to different camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Well, this is when now, about 1942, '43?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're busy trying to inflict as much damage--

speaker: interviewee
text: Trying to survive and-- and do the most damage we-- we could do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who would plan these maneuvers? Did you sit down with the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they would say here--

speaker: interviewee
text: They would say here is a good spot where to blow up the train because it was a curving area, where the-- where the train had to curve. And that's usually a good area because when you blow it up, it comes-- pulls all the rest of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cars with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cars with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do with what was on the train? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: If it was ammunition there, we took the ammunition with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was usually on there, ammunition? Or was there ever any troops?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes there were troops.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They shot back at us, and we had a battle on our hands. That's what it us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you-- I mean, you were involved in basically trying to eliminate them and shooting them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any hand-to-hand combat where you were involved with anybody in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not hand-to-hand, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how-- go ahead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost quite a-- a number of-- of good people there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away from Dubno was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 200 miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you move around, just on foot?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had wagons and horses. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Horses and wagons-- we moved around with horses and wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear about anything that was going on regarding to the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't hear anything on the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were basically kind of cut off from the outside world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were cut off from the outside world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear any  stories about what happened in Dubno when they massacred the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we heard some stories. Because they were coming with trucks and loading up the-- the peoples that  were in ghetto, taking them out to the airport, where they were digging-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Ditches?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ditches. They-- they-- they dug out ditches, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 10 feet deep and 20 feet wide and 30 feet long. And when-- when we still were in the ghetto, we inquired about these ditches. So they said they were going to store potatoes in them for the winter. That's what they told us.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the meantime, they used it to shoot people.

speaker: interviewee
text: They used it to shoot the population in there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me more, in terms of what you did in some of your other activities. I mean, how many trains a week did you blow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: At least one a week. At least one a week. We had to do some reconnaissance and find out at what times they're going by a certain area. And I was pretty good at it. So I usually used to go and get the reconnaissance, how many trains go by a certain area where it was beneficial for us to-- to blow up that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would you talk to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Local population.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever anybody that you had infiltrators inside your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have any infiltrators. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Any particular times that you can remember specifically about when you thought you might be killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- I-- I was not afraid of being killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because all my family was killed already. I didn't have nothing to live for. So I was not afraid of being killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was when? 1943?

speaker: interviewee
text: '43. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically, until liberation, you were doing this kind of work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Once a week, you'd figure out where to blow up a train.

speaker: interviewee
text: At least once a week we-- we'd pick a target. And then we-- we go out to that area and scout it out, which way was the best way to approach it and where-- where-- which area to blow it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And didn't the Germans ever catch on? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: By the time they-- they caught on, we were far away from that area because we used to change camps very often. About every three or four days, we used to change camps. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We have four minutes left on this tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, in terms of a camp, can you describe what it was like setting up camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we picked areas where-- where water was close by and where we-- we wouldn't be spotted from the air. So we picked areas like that where it was water close by.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people are in your group at this time?

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody had a job to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody had a job to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yours was blowing up the trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other kind of jobs were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: To assemble food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had-- we had to spot-- spot it out where-- where we could get some food for 200 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever bring food or anything on the trains that you could ever get into? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mostly ammunition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ammunitions. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How big were these trains? How many cars usually?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 20 carloads. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not going to say the wrong thing to him.
